tranches.goal: establish bounded first-pass routing and frozen-reading basics
tranches.why_here: later control layers are harder to read if baseline routing and corpus role boundaries do not already exist
should_not_precede: later anti-confusion, claim, or promotion-history surfaces

tranches.goal: prevent early public misreading of terms, scope, and objections
tranches.why_here: claims and status are easier to read once confusion and citation posture are already bounded
should_not_precede: later claim, ownership, or handoff surfaces

tranches.goal: expose what the corpus claims and how strongly or maturely it should be read
tranches.why_here: public readers need claims, maturity, and reading force together before later governance layers make sense
should_not_precede: later open-question, handoff, or promotion-history layers

tranches.goal: establish maintenance and reading-discipline rules for the visible public layer
tranches.why_here: once claims are public, the maintenance rules for keeping them coherent should become visible

tranches.goal: fix canonical home, governing-source order, and invariant safety
tranches.why_here: boundary and authority questions are clearer after orientation, anti-confusion, claims, and maintenance rules already exist
should_not_precede: artifact, handoff, or later promotion-planning layers

tranches.goal: stabilize artifact identity and the path for new package admission
tranches.why_here: identity and intake decisions are easier to read once ownership and precedence are already visible
should_not_precede: later promotion-history and outward handoff layers

tranches.goal: bound public-safe exposure, audience paths, and outward bundle sufficiency
tranches.why_here: outward-facing routing should come after earlier framing, authority, and identity layers are already public

tranches.goal: make local/public history discipline explicit once earlier supporting control surfaces already exist
tranches.why_here: promotion-state and atomic-bundle rules are safer once the earlier control layer can already support them
should_not_precede: the supporting control surfaces they depend on

tranches.goal: show deliberate incompleteness without letting it masquerade as silent closure
tranches.why_here: open territory is easier to read once claims, status, authority, and boundary framing are already public
should_not_precede: the stronger framing that shows what is already claimed and what is not

tranches.goal: allow later cleanup, index tightening, or bounded consolidation after prior tranches are already public
minimum_surfaces: short later cleanup or consolidation pass only after prior tranche visibility exists
tranches.why_here: consolidation is least misleading once the earlier public-history layers are already visible in order

anti_patterns.problem: promoting late control layers before basic entry orientation exists
anti_patterns.what_goes_wrong: public history starts with downstream control wording but no stable first-pass route
anti_patterns.why_harmful: later readers meet conclusions before they can even identify the corpus map

anti_patterns.problem: promoting claims/status without citation and routing clarity
anti_patterns.what_goes_wrong: claims appear before citation posture and anti-confusion framing are visible
anti_patterns.why_harmful: public readers cannot tell what the claims point to or how narrowly they should be read

anti_patterns.problem: promoting ownership/precedence without earlier anti-confusion control
anti_patterns.what_goes_wrong: authority and canonical-home language appears before confusion boundaries are fixed
anti_patterns.why_harmful: public history begins to look like internal re-ordering without the framing needed to interpret it

anti_patterns.problem: promoting handoff/export before public-safe boundary exists
anti_patterns.what_goes_wrong: outward bundle logic appears before safety and exclusion classes are public
anti_patterns.why_harmful: readers may infer that outward sharing rules are broader or safer than the corpus actually established

anti_patterns.problem: promoting promotion policy before acceptance/sync layer exists
anti_patterns.what_goes_wrong: local/public state language appears before the maintenance rules that justify it
anti_patterns.why_harmful: public history can look procedurally authoritative without the earlier control discipline

anti_patterns.problem: promoting open-question layer before stronger claims/status framing exists
anti_patterns.what_goes_wrong: unresolved territory appears before the corpus shows what is already claimed and how mature it is
anti_patterns.why_harmful: non-closure becomes harder to interpret and may look like vagueness rather than deliberate restraint

anti_patterns.problem: promoting later tranches in isolation so public history appears conceptually backward
anti_patterns.what_goes_wrong: later history-facing or boundary-facing layers arrive without their earlier supports
anti_patterns.why_harmful: the visible sequence starts to misrepresent conceptual dependence inside the corpus

anti_patterns.problem: mixing unrelated tranche content into one noisy undifferentiated promotion step
anti_patterns.what_goes_wrong: public history receives too many unrelated control moves at once
anti_patterns.why_harmful: reviewers lose the ability to see what changed first, why, and which layer actually depends on which

fail_closed_rule: If maintainers cannot determine whether a local layer belongs to an earlier or later tranche, default to the earlier supporting tranche logic before promotion.
